rationale: Individuals who experience trauma often develop shock as a result of the major stress placed on the body by the traumatic event/injury. Signs of early shock include respiratory alkalosis on ABGs (as a result of hyperventilation and bronchodilation as the body attempts to increase tissue perfusion/oxygenation); a decrease in urine output (in an attempt to preserve the brain and heart, the body will shift perfusion from the kidneys and other abdominal organs in early shock); and narrowed pulse pressure (as a result of systemic vasoconstriction mediated by the sympathetic nervous system), among many other signs. Late shock is considered to be noncompensatory and progressive, with many patients not recovering due to the irreversible cycle of late stage shock. Patients in late stage shock often have breath sounds which sound congested (due to the presence of pulmonary edema), shallow respirations, decreased pulse oximetry readings, severe hypotension with tachycardia, renal failure, and metabolic acidosis on ABGs.

stem: You are working as the CFRN and are called to transport a ventilated patient to another facility. Upon arrival to the patient, you find the patient is on SIMV ventilator setting. Which of the following best describes the SIMV vent setting?
options: SIMV is a setting that allows the patient to trigger a breath. Every breath, whether triggered by the patient or the ventilator, receives a full set tidal volume.
SIMV is a setting that delivers a constant positive pressure during inspiration and expiration.
SIMV is a setting that does not allow a patient to take a spontaneous breath. All breaths are delivered by the ventilator.

SIMV is a setting that allows spontaneous breathing by the patient. During the respiratory time interval, the ventilator will either deliver a breath or allow a breath triggered by the patient. If the patient triggers a breath, the ventilator will provide pressure support. If no breath is triggered, the ventilator will deliver a breath at the set rate.
answer: SIMV is a setting that allows spontaneous breathing by the patient. During the respiratory time interval, the ventilator will either deliver a breath or allow a breath triggered by the patient. If the patient triggers a breath, the ventilator will provide pressure support. If no breath is triggered, the ventilator will deliver a breath at the set rate.
rationale: SIMV is a setting that allows spontaneous breathing by the patient. During the respiratory time interval, the ventilator will either deliver a breath or allow a breath triggered by the patient. If the patient triggers a breath, the ventilator will provide pressure support. If no breath is triggered, the ventilator will deliver a breath at the set rate. A setting that does not allow a patient to take a spontaneous breath describes CMV. AC is a setting that allows the patient to trigger a breath or a breath is triggered by the determined rate and a full tidal volume is delivered. A setting that delivers constant pressure during inspiration and expiration defines CPAP.

stem: In addition to its analgesic properties, Ketamine also has which of the following properties?
rationale: Ketamine (Ketalar) is a medication used for induction of RSI. It contains hypnotic, analgesic, and amnestic properties. Ketamine also is a potent beta 2 agonist causing bronchodilation.

stem: You are responsible for placing a nasopharyngeal airway in a stuporous patient who requires only minimal airway support. When placing the airway using the left nare, which of the following descriptors most accurately describes the insertion technique which should be used?
options: Lifting the tip of the nose, insert the lubricated airway device into the nare with the beveled edge against the inside of the nostril, moving the device through the nasal passage into the posterior pharynx using gentle pressure
Insert the airway device upside down into the nare with the beveled edge against the septum and then rotate the device once the tip is located in the posterior pharynx
Insert the airway device in the same manner used when inserting the device into the right nare
Lubricate the airway device, inserting it upright with the beveled edge against the septum, then slowly glide the device into place with the tip located in the posterior pharynx
answer: Insert the airway device upside down into the nare with the beveled edge against the septum and then rotate the device once the tip is located in the posterior pharynx
rationale: A nasopharyngeal airway may be used to maintain airway patency in stuporous patients who require only minimal airway support. Appropriate size choice is important to both prevent injury to the nasal passages and pharynx, as well as to ensure an appropriate airway is actually being maintained. Once the correct size has been determined, the nasopharyngeal airway should be adequately lubricated and, if being inserted into the left nare, the device should be inserted upside down into the nare, placing the beveled edge against the nasal septum. Once the tip of the device reaches the posterior pharynx, it should then be rotated into correct position.

stem: You are part of a critical care transport team preparing to transfer an intubated patient from the emergency department to a tertiary care center. The patient requires continuous invasive blood pressure monitoring during transport. An arterial line is in place prior to departure. Which of the following statements regarding arterial lines is most accurate in this situation?
options: Arterial line site selection is standardized and does not vary based on patient condition or transport considerations
Arterial lines are primarily used to evaluate preload and right-sided cardiac function
Arterial lines must be set up with a pressure bag and a continuous flush device
Once placed, arterial lines do not require pressurization if waveform quality is adequate
answer: Arterial lines must be set up with a pressure bag and a continuous flush device
rationale: Arterial lines provide continuous invasive blood pressure monitoring and allow for accurate measurement of mean arterial pressure (MAP) during transport. A pressurized flush system with a continuous low-rate flush is required to maintain catheter patency and prevent thrombus formation. This setup is essential to preserve waveform fidelity in the transport environment. Arterial lines measure systemic arterial pressure and reflect left-sided circulatory dynamics. Assessment of preload or right-sided cardiac function requires central venous or pulmonary artery monitoring, not arterial catheterization. Arterial lines require continuous pressurization regardless of the initial waveform. Without a pressure bag and flush device, clot formation and waveform damping can occur, leading to inaccurate blood pressure readings. Arterial line site selection varies based on patient anatomy, injury pattern, infection risk, and anticipated transport challenges. While the radial artery is commonly used, alternative sites may be selected based on clinical circumstances.

stem: The patient you are packaging to prepare for medical air transport has an arterial line in place for hemodynamic monitoring. Once the patient has been loaded into the fixed-wing aircraft, your partner performs a thorough assessment of the patient while you are responsible for maintenance and reading of the arterial line. You prepare to level the system, using the phlebostatic axis as the zero reference point. Where is the phlebostatic axis located?
options: At the stopcock closest to the transducer
At the head of the bed when elevated to 45 degrees
rationale: Medical air transport crews will frequently transport patients who have invasive hemodynamic monitoring devices in place, including arterial lines, central venous catheters, and pulmonary artery catheters. All medical transport crew members need to be highly-skilled in the maintenance and monitoring of these devices, as well as in troubleshooting potential complications of use of hemodynamic monitoring. The most frequent errors in use of invasive hemodynamic monitoring is the incorrect/inaccurate leveling (or zeroing) of the device. The air-fluid interface of the stopcock closest to the transducer should be placed at the phlebostatic axis for leveling. The phlebostatic axis is located at the 4th intercostal space, which is generally considered to be the level of the atria. Once the transducer is placed at the phlebostatic axis, the stopcock should be shut off to the patient, and the device should be "zeroed." Incorrectly placing the transducer at the phlebostatic axis or misidentification of the site of the phlebostatic axis can result in 2 mm Hg errors in readings for every 1-inch of site discrepancy.

stem: You are the CFRN transporting a trauma patient from a rural hospital to a regional trauma center. The patient is sedated and intubated. The patient has fentanyl and versed drips hanging for sedation and is receiving blood products as well. While en route to the receiving facility, you note that the patient is breaking out in hives. The patient begins to show sinus tachycardia on the monitor and the latest blood pressure is 80/45. What action should you perform?
options: Provide more sedation for pain management
rationale: The signs of rash, tachycardia, and hypotension all point to anaphylactic reaction. When this occurs during blood transfusion, the provider must stop the transfusion and treat the anaphylactic shock. Administering more pain medication would be contraindicated in this scenario due to hypotension. While a rapid fluid bolus may briefly help the hypotension, the main cause of the symptoms is a transfusion reaction and the blood transfusion must be stopped. Decreasing the patient's PEEP will not help in this scenario.

stem: The GCS is commonly used in the pre-hospital and acute care setting, as well as over a patient's hospital course, to evaluate mental status in both traumatic and non-traumatic presentations. Which of the following are components of calculating a GCS?
rationale: The purpose of the Glasgow Coma Score scale is to describe and communicate the condition of an individual patient by separate, multidimensional rating of their eye, verbal, and motor responses. It remains the appropriate method for this purpose. It is calculated by addition of the total points selected under each component (eye, verbal, motor), e.g., "15 points".

stem: You are called to transport a patient from home who has experienced hemorrhagic shock as a result of massive gastrointestinal bleeding due to intestinal perforation by cancerous tumors. The patient has a systolic blood pressure of 80, a heart rate of 135 beats/min, a respiratory rate of 35 breaths/min, and appears to be confused. In what class of shock state is this patient?
rationale: Hypovolemic shock occurs as a result of a significant decrease in the circulating intravascular volume. This may be due to dehydration, or in the case of the patient in this scenario, due to hemorrhagic blood loss. Hypovolemic/hemorrhagic shock has been divided into 4 classes of shock to aid in recognition of the degree of both blood loss and disability, specifically neurologic disability. Shock class can be determined by estimation of the amount of blood loss, as well as by assessment of physiologic parameters, both of which are inextricably linked to each other. Shock classes are defined as follows: Class I shock: EBL \< 750 mLs, with minimal (or no) change noted in BP, HR, or RR. Class II shock: EBL 750--1500 mLs; HR > 100, RR elevated between 20--30 breaths/minute, patient typically appears anxious. BP remains within normal range. Class III shock: EBL 1500--2000 mLs; HR > 120, RR between 30--40 breaths/minute, patient may appear confused. BP is now decreased. Class IV shock: EBL > 2000 mLs; HR > 140, RR > 35, BP remains decreased, and the patient is lethargic or unresponsive.

stem: Select the ventilator setting in which the patient has no ability to initiate their own breath.
rationale: In this setting, all breaths are triggered, limited, and cycled by the ventilator. The patient has no control to initiate their own breaths. This setting is used for sedated and paralyzed patients.

stem: Which of the following statements regarding placement of an intravenous (IV) line in a non-hemorrhaging trauma patient requiring medical transport is most accurate?
options: Placement of an IV line is necessary for correction of the patient's underlying problem
Placement of an IV line during transport allows for minimal delay if the patient requires surgical intervention
Vascular access should be secured prior to transport
Cannulating the internal jugular vein will facilitate delivery of improved care during transport
answer: Placement of an IV line during transport allows for minimal delay if the patient requires surgical intervention
rationale: Primary assessment of the trauma patient includes assessing of the patient's circulation and control of any hemorrhage. As part of this assessment is included placement of an intravenous (IV) line for delivery of any necessary medications, IV fluids, and blood products. While placement of an IV line is of critical importance when providing medical transport of trauma patients, the medical transport provider should remember that placement of an IV line does not correct a patient's problem, it solely is used for provision of supplemental therapy. Inserting an endotracheal tube (ETT) and providing mechanical ventilation serve to actually correct underlying problems encountered in trauma patients. In an ideal situation, every trauma patient would have a readily-available IV line, but this is often not the case. Transport of the trauma patient should not be delayed to allow for placement of IV access, unless the patient's condition requires the immediate administration of medications (as for rapid sequence intubation) or blood products. In recent years, the medical transport provider has gained the ability to place central line catheters, such as internal jugular lines; while this allows for excellent vascular access, placement of a central line catheter may require multiple attempts, delaying transport of the patient. Transport of the patient should not be delayed; placement of alternate, more easily obtainable intravenous access (such as placement of an intraosseous line) should be attempted.

stem: As part of the medical air transport team, you are given a mission to transport a patient who sustained trauma as a result of a boating accident. Which of the following patient findings, if noted during the primary assessment, would indicate an increased risk for aspiration in the patient?
options: The presence of a tourniquet placed by the ground team due to traumatic amputation
core temperature reading of 35 degrees Celsius
rationale: As part of the primary assessment, the medical transport team should be assessing for "disability," that is, assessing the neurologic status of the patient (this is the "D" of the "ABCDE" mnemonic). This component of the primary assessment should be completed rapidly, and can be facilitated by the use of the mnemonic "AVPU," which stands for Alert, responds to Verbal stimuli, responds to Painful stimuli, or Unresponsive. In addition, the neurologic assessment includes assessment of eye movement (spontaneous or only in response to stimuli), pupillary response and symmetry of the pupils, any noted extremity movements, and the presence of any motor findings which are lateralized. With the information gathered in the neurologic assessment, the medical transport provider can assign a Glasgow Coma Scale score; a score of 8 or less indicates the patient is at an increased risk of aspiration and hypoventilation. The medical transport provider should consider placement of an emergency airway in a patient who has a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 8 or less. Temperature taking is not included as part of the primary assessment; it is, however, a component of the secondary assessment.

stem: What is the difference in volume versus pressure when in relation to ventilator delivery methods?
options: Volume: a preset tidal volume is delivered by the ventilator. After the set volume is given, the exhalation phase begins. Volumes are consistent every breath. Pressure: a preset inspiratory pressure is given by the ventilator. Once the pressure is achieved, the exhalation phase begins. Volumes can change from breath to breath.
The only difference between volume and pressure is the units used to measure the amount of air given.
Volume: tidal volume is determined by a preset amount of pressure. Pressure: pressure is determined by a preset amount of volume.

Volume: a preset pressure is delivered by the ventilator. After pressure is achieved, the exhalation begins. Volumes can change from breath to breath. Pressure: a preset tidal volume is delivered by the ventilator. After the set volume is given, the exhalation phase begins. Volumes are consistent every breath.
answer: Volume: a preset tidal volume is delivered by the ventilator. After the set volume is given, the exhalation phase begins. Volumes are consistent every breath. Pressure: a preset inspiratory pressure is given by the ventilator. Once the pressure is achieved, the exhalation phase begins. Volumes can change from breath to breath.
rationale: Volume and pressure are both settings that can be set by the CFRN. When controlling volume, each breath delivered will be at the chosen volume (i.e., 450 mL). The pressure between each breath can change due to lung compliance and if the equipment is in working order. It is imperative the CFRN monitor pressures when delivering set volumes. If the CFRN elects to use pressure delivery mode, the ventilator will deliver each breath up to a certain preset pressure (i.e., 30 cm H₂O). Once the pressure has been delivered, the exhalation phase begins. In this setting, the volumes of air delivered will vary and therefore, minute volume must be monitored to ensure the patient is adequately ventilated.

stem: Unless otherwise indicated, initial ventilator settings should include which positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP)?
rationale: PEEP exerts pressure in a patient's airways above the atmospheric level throughout the respiratory cycle, increasing the functional residual capacity (FRC) by opening collapsed alveoli. The ideal PEEP setting prevents cyclic atelectasis and overdistention injury while optimizing oxygenation. Most patients benefit from the addition of 5 cm H₂O PEEP to overcome the decrease in FRC as a result of the airway resistance caused by the endotracheal tube (ETT).

stem: Which of the following narcotic analgesics is most commonly administered as a pretreatment medication for patients preparing to undergo rapid sequence intubation (RSI)?
rationale: Fentanyl is the drug of choice when needing to provide analgesic pretreatment prior to RSI. A release of catecholamines occurs during RSI, causing a subsequent rise in blood pressure, heart rate, and intracranial pressure. For patients whose condition would be significantly impaired or negatively affected by these physiologic increases, such as patients who have sustained head injuries or in patients with cardiovascular disease, pretreatment with fentanyl may be indicated. Fentanyl has a rapid onset (3 to 5 minutes) and short duration of action (30 to 60 minutes), and generally is the best choice for RSI. Risks include potential of causing chest wall rigidity (typically with higher administration doses) which can make ventilation difficult, and exacerbation of hypotension.

stem: Implementing noninvasive positive pressure ventilation (NiPPV) during transport is recommended for patients with which condition?
rationale: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema is characterized by fluid accumulation in the lungs due to heart failure, leading to respiratory distress. Noninvasive positive pressure ventilation (NiPPV) is beneficial for such patients as it improves oxygenation, reduces the work of breathing, and helps to decrease pulmonary edema by reducing cardiac preload and afterload. This therapy is particularly advantageous because it avoids the need for invasive intubation during transport. Hemodynamic instability is a contraindication for NiPPV because it often requires immediate intubation and mechanical ventilation to stabilize the patient. The inability to control the airway poses a high aspiration risk, making NiPPV unsuitable. Patients unable to maintain their airway require immediate intubation to secure ventilation. Unstable arrhythmia is a contraindication for NiPPV because it may exacerbate the underlying condition and necessitate alternative stabilization measures, including invasive mechanical ventilation.

stem: A patient you are caring for has a patent airway, but his respirations are ineffective for adequate ventilation. What should you do first?
options: Provide ventilations via bag valve mask
Place a supraglottic airway device such as an LMA
answer: Provide ventilations via bag valve mask
rationale: Upon recognizing a patient has either inadequate or ineffective spontaneous respirations, the CFRN should initiate positive pressure ventilations via a bag valve mask (BVM). Prior to performing any advanced airway technique, patients must be oxygenated with the goal of SpO2 of 100%. The only way to accomplish this with a patient who has inadequate respirations is to provide positive pressure ventilations (PPV) with high flow oxygen.

stem: Which of the following statements most accurately describes continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) ventilation?
options: ventilation method in which the patient breathes spontaneously with continuously maintained PEEP
ventilation method that employs the use of alternating levels of PEEP
ventilation method unique to a specific ventilator manufacturer
answer: ventilation method in which the patient breathes spontaneously with continuously maintained PEEP
rationale: Invasive continuous airway pressure (CPAP) is a method of ventilation during which a patient is intubated but allowed to breathe spontaneously while receiving a low level of continuous positive airway pressure to maintain PEEP (positive end expiratory pressure). The use of invasive CPAP should be supported through the use of a volume-controlled, pressure-controlled method of ventilation. The continuous pressure delivered by CPAP aids in keeping the smaller airways and alveoli open, and also opens any collapsed alveoli, improving the functional residual capacity (FRC) and preventing atelectasis. Bi-level continuous positive airway pressure utilizes the delivery of alternating levels of PEEP, and if referred to as "BiPAP," refers to a specific manufacturer versus a ventilator setting.

stem: Which of the following vasopressor medications is most appropriate for use in adult patients experiencing shock?
rationale: The use of vasopressors in the treatment of shock state should be standard of care, regardless of the cause of the shock state, if hypotension does not respond to the use of intravenous fluids in an attempt to bolster systemic blood pressure. Stimulation of the sympathetic nervous system through the use of adrenergic agonists is considered first-line treatment in the treatment of hypotension due to shock state. Both alpha- and beta-adrenergic drugs may be utilized, and their rapid onset of action is crucial to patient survival. While norepinephrine, dopamine, epinephrine, and vasopressin may all be utilized in an attempt to improve systemic blood pressure during shock state, only norepinephrine consistently demonstrates safe, effective improvements in both peripheral vasomotor tone and cardiac output without affording additional risk to the patient. For these reasons, norepinephrine is considered the best first-choice vasopressor for use in most adult shock patients. Dopamine should not be used as a first-line vasopressor due to possibility of contributing to arrhythmias, but may be considered as a second-line, or adjunct, vasopressor. Epinephrine is most often used as an adjunct agent for refractory states of shock. Vasopressin should only be used in shock patients with ample, if not high, cardiac output.

stem: A potential risk of giving succinylcholine is malignant hyperthermia (MH). Which medication should be administered if the CFRN notices signs of MH?
rationale: Malignant hyperthermia is a rare but fatal condition that sometimes is subsequent of succinylcholine administration. The correct medication to give is Dantrolene Sodium (Dantrium). Calcium channel blockers are contraindicated for MH.

stem: You are the CFRN caring for a recently intubated trauma patient. The patient's vital signs are HR-104 sinus rhythm, BP-143/87, respirations-12 (ventilated), and SPO₂ of 100%. Which medication should you consider for post intubation management?
rationale: Ensuring adequate sedation for a patient post intubation is essential. Ketamine is the only answer choice that has analgesic properties and is a sedative. Etomidate is used as an induction agent for its quick onset; however, the medication's half-life is very short, making it less than ideal to maintain sedation. Also, Etomidate has no analgesic properties. While the patient may be unresponsive, they are still able to experience pain and discomfort. Succinylcholine and Rocuronium are both neuromuscular blocking agents and have no analgesic or anesthetic properties. In fact, there have been reported instances when patients are administered only NMBA and recall every part of the intubation procedure.

stem: The medical air transport program is providing training on the use of noninvasive positive pressure ventilation (NiPPV). Which of the following patients would be best served by the use of NiPPV during medical air transport?
options: patient experiencing an acute exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)
patient that is at risk for respiratory arrest
patient with an unstable cardiac arrhythmia
patient who is in organ failure
answer: patient experiencing an acute exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)
rationale: The use of noninvasive positive pressure ventilation (NiPPV) during medical air transport can be applied to patients experiencing respiratory complications as a result of both acute and chronic conditions. This being said, NiPPV should be used with caution during medical air transport, as successful application and use of this method are reliant on skilled crew members who are expert in the delivery of mechanical ventilation. During NiPPV, the patient is not sedated, or only lightly sedated, and requires constant monitoring to ensure adequate ventilation is being delivered. Typically, ventilation is delivered through the use of a tight-fitting face or nasal mask, and if correctly administered and delivered, may prevent (or delay) the need for endotracheal intubation. Patients who are experiencing acute COPD exacerbation or acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema are excellent candidates for the use of NiPPV. It may also be used in those patients who need, but refuse, to be intubated. NiPPV should not be used in patients at risk for cardiac or respiratory arrest or who are experiencing unstable cardiac arrhythmias; those who are hemodynamically unstable; patients at risk of aspiration or who have upper airway obstruction; patients who have facial trauma or who have facial deformities; or patients who are in organ failure (not respiratory failure).

stem: You are the CFRN caring for a patient in respiratory distress secondary to suspected pneumonia. You initiate continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy via nasal prongs. How will the effectiveness of this therapy be directly measured?
rationale: In the transport environment, a nonrebreather, CPAP, or another non-invasive mode of ventilation may be required for a patient in respiratory distress. CPAP improves oxygenation by increasing lung volume and improving V/Q matching, which decreases the work of breathing. CPAP delivers constant minimal positive pressure, thus preventing upper airway and alveolar collapse. Oxygenation is directly measured via the PO₂ of an ABG level. If the patient deteriorates and advances to severe respiratory distress or failure, they may require noninvasive ventilation or advanced airway management with an ETT and mechanical ventilation. The other choices do not give direct measurements of oxygenation.

stem:
You are called by a ground crew at a scene as an additional ALS resource. The patient is an 84-year-old female with COPD and CHF who called EMS for shortness of breath and chest pain. The patient has a DNI (Do Not Intubate) order. You are the CFRN, and the ground medic gives you patient report and hands the appropriate paperwork to you.

Vitals are as follows:
HR: 87 and weak
NIBP: 150/91; Respirations-28 labored
SpO₂: 81%

Patient's lung sounds reveal crackles in the bases, bilaterally. The patient is awake and alert. Patient is able to answer questions appropriately but in 1 to 2 word sentences. What airway intervention should be performed?
options: No intervention is needed at this time since the patient is awake and alert
Place an oropharyngeal airway (OPA) in the patient
Ignore the DNI. Since you are an additional resource called to the scene with higher training, the DNI does not apply to you.
rationale: This patient is most likely experiencing COPD exacerbation and has some pulmonary edema, given her lung sounds and history of CHF. This patient is in obvious respiratory distress. The patient has a DNI, and that must be respected regardless of the level of the provider giving care. The patient will not tolerate an OPA since she is awake and alert. The needed intervention is to place the patient on CPAP. This will do two things: open the airways, and the PEEP will move fluid in the alveoli back into the pulmonary circulation. This will allow better ventilation and perfusion to occur.

stem: While transporting a ventilated patient, you notice a low pressure ventilator alarm. The patient's capnography no longer shows a waveform, and the patient's SpO2 drops from 99% to 70%. What is the most likely cause?
options: The patient is developing a pneumothorax
rationale: In this scenario, we need to remember the DOPE acronym for reasons of airway compromise. DOPE stands for Dislodge ETT, Obstructed ETT, Pneumothorax, Equipment failure. Flash pulmonary edema is not one of the considerations. ETT displacement, pneumothorax, and mucus plug obstruction could explain why the patient SpO2 decreased. However, if the patient had a pneumothorax, we could still see waveform capnography, and the ventilator alarm would alarm high pressure, not low as stated in the question. Obstruction would also give a high pressure alarm, therefore, ETT dislodgement is the best answer.

stem: Of the following, which method is the most commonly utilized for provision of invasive airway management across all age groups?
rationale: Orotracheal intubation, otherwise known as endotracheal intubation, is the most common method of intubation used to provide emergency airway management for patients across the lifespan. Contraindications to orotracheal intubation are uncommon, if not completely absent, when this technique is used to secure the airway in a true emergency situation. The use of nasotracheal intubation in the field has largely been replaced by the use of rapid sequence intubation (RSI). Cricothyrotomy is typically utilized in the field only if orotracheal intubation has been unsuccessful, or if adequate oxygen saturation (above 90%) is unable to be maintained through the use of orotracheal intubation. A supraglottic device, such as a laryngeal mask airway (LMA), may be used in the field to obtain a rapid airway, however, the risk of aspiration of gastric contents is much greater with use of these devices.

stem: While there is no single way to ensure that an airway appliance is properly placed during transport, what is the most accurate and easily available method to monitor correct endotracheal tube (ETT) positioning?
options: Auscultating breath sounds in all lung fields
Observing the rise and fall of the patient's chest
Visualizing the ETT through the vocal cords
rationale: Continuous end-tidal CO₂ detection with the use of capnometry is the standard of care for any patient with an airway appliance during transport. It is highly reliable for pulseless patients with high-quality cardiopulmonary resuscitation. Disposable qualitative end-tidal CO₂ detectors evaluate proper ETT placement by incorporating a nontoxic, pH-sensitive chemically treated indicator that changes color in the presence of carbon dioxide.

stem: You are a CFRN called to do an inter-facility transport of a 67-year-old female in respiratory distress. Which ABG values would indicate that the patient is in respiratory failure?
options: pH = 7.12, CO2 = 42 mm Hg, PaO2 = 62 mm Hg
pH = 7.14, CO2 = 62 mm Hg, PaO2 = 59 mm Hg
pH = 7.44, CO2 = 36 mm Hg, PaO2 = 80 mm Hg
pH = 7.50, CO2 = 32 mm Hg, PaO2 = 120 mm Hg
answer: pH = 7.14, CO2 = 62 mm Hg, PaO2 = 59 mm Hg
rationale: Respiratory failure (RF) is diagnosed when the patient loses the ability to ventilate adequately or to provide sufficient oxygen to the blood and systemic organs. RF is defined as: PO2 \< 60 mm Hg. It is frequently accompanied by ventilatory failure (CO2 > 50 mm hg). Urgent resuscitation of the patient requires airway control, ventilatory management, and stabilization of the circulation, while providing treatment for the underlying cause.

stem: Which of the following medications, commonly used in airway management, is the only depolarizing neuromuscular-blocking agent (NMBA) in use?
rationale: All NMBAs work at the level of the neuromuscular end plate, disrupting neurotransmitter (acetylcholine) function and preventing effective contraction of skeletal muscle. These agents do not produce analgesia, anesthesia, or amnesia. For this reason, sedation of the patient is essential before and during extended periods of NMBA use. Succinylcholine opens sodium channels, causing prolonged depolarization and uncoordinated muscle movements or fasciculation; it is the only depolarizing NMBA in use. Vecuronium and pancuronium are nondepolarizing NMBAs. Propofol is an intravenous anesthetic used for procedural sedation.

stem: Which of the following medications is not commonly used during resuscitation in cardiopulmonary arrest?
rationale: Advanced cardiac life support (ACLS) guidelines are used as the standard of practice for resuscitation of the patient in cardiac arrest by most transport programs. Team members are expected to have current verification of ACLS skills through the American Heart Association (AHA) to participate in patient care within the transport environment. Transport teams must maintain a state of perpetual readiness for emergencies such as cardiac arrest. Preparation includes ensuring the proper functioning of resuscitation equipment and an adequate oxygen supply that is readily available. ACLS drugs should be well labeled, not expired, and ready for quick administration. The drugs most commonly used during resuscitation include epinephrine, lidocaine, and amiodarone. Dobutamine has not been clinically shown to improve cardiopulmonary arrest outcomes.

stem: You are participating in the field rescue of an individual whom you suspect has sustained a cervical spine injury due to involvement in a motor vehicle accident. The injured individual is experiencing difficulty with spontaneous ventilation, and you plan to intubate. Which method of airway management/intubation is preferred for this individual?
rationale: The use of video-assisted laryngoscopy (VL) is recommended when placing an endotracheal tube (ETT) in patients who have suspected cervical spine injuries or other neck mobility concerns. VL is more gentle than use of direct laryngoscopy (DL); the use of a supraglottic airway (SGA) device would also be appropriate for this patient. Nasal intubation is infrequently utilized today although historically, it was considered the method-of-choice when airway management was needed for patients with cervical spine injuries.

stem: Which of the following is not considered to be a basic life support airway intervention?
rationale: All patient care begins with an evaluation of the primary assessment, which involves ensuring that the airway is patent and that the patient is breathing adequately. If either of these conditions is unmet, a basic life support intervention is life-saving. Airway patency can nearly always be re-established with basic interventions such as: patient positioning (unless precluded by an injury involving the cervical spine) the use of a suction device the use of an oral and/or nasal airway adjunct Patients who require basic life support interventions (for their airway or breathing) for more than a few minutes will generally require more advanced interventions, such as a cricothyroidotomy, for longer-term management.

stem: You are providing medical air transport care for a patient in shock and note that the patient's skin is cool, moist and pale above the nipple line and warm and dry below. What type of shock is this patient most likely experiencing?
rationale: Neurogenic shock, a type of distributive shock, results in vasomotor dysfunction as a result of spinal cord injury. Loss of sympathetic nervous system tone ensues with the loss of spinal cord transmission, resulting in a loss of vascular tone below the level of the spinal injury. Patients in neurogenic shock will often present with cool, moist, pale skin (often consistent with other types of shock, such as hypovolemic or cardiogenic shock) above the level of spinal injury, while the skin below the level of spinal injury is warm, dry, and sometimes flushed (as is seen in septic and anaphylactoid shock).

stem: You are the CFRN called to the scene of an 87-year-old female having stroke-like symptoms. Upon arrival to the scene, you and your partner determine that the patient is in need of a secure airway and you elect to perform Rapid Sequence Intubation. The patient's family tells you that the patient weighs 70 kg. Which is the correct dose of paralytic?
rationale: The correct dosage of succinylcholine is 1.0--1.5 mg/kg adult dose. Since the patient weighs 70 kg, the correct dose of succinylcholine is 70.0 mg. A dose of 700.0 mg of succinylcholine is ten times the adult dose for this size patient. The same is true for a dose of 700.0 mg of rocuronium. The adult dose of rocuronium is 0.5--1.0 mg/kg. Rocuronium 0.50 mg is too small a dose.

stem: Which of the following is/are complications associated with administering Fentanyl too rapidly or in too high of a dosage?
rationale: The critical care transport provider should be mindful of the complication of chest wall rigidity when using fentanyl as a premedication in RSI. Chest wall rigidity can make ventilation impossible. Administering Fentanyl does not cause malignant hyperthermia. Fentanyl can cause respiratory depression and hypotension when given in large enough doses.

stem: What is the recommended tidal volume (Vₜ) for mechanically ventilated patients to prevent acute lung injury (ALI) during transport?
options: 10 to 15 mL/kg of ideal body weight (IBW)
6 to 8 mL/kg of ideal body weight (IBW)
answer: 6 to 8 mL/kg of ideal body weight (IBW)
rationale: The higher the tidal volume in a mechanically ventilated patient is, the greater the risk of ALI becomes during transport. A landmark study published in 2000 showed that lower Vₜ led to a decrease in mortality and established a new norm for mechanical ventilation Vₜ of 6 to 8 mL/kg of IBW.

stem: What is the mechanism of action of epinephrine during attempted resuscitation?
options: Epinephrine stimulates spontaneous contractions in the presence of asystole
Epinephrine reduces peripheral vascular resistance and myocardial afterload
answer: Epinephrine stimulates spontaneous contractions in the presence of asystole
rationale: Epinephrine stimulates alpha-, beta1-, and beta2-adrenergic receptors, resulting in relaxation of smooth muscle of the bronchial tree, cardiac stimulation (increasing myocardial oxygen consumption), and dilation of skeletal muscle vasculature; small doses can cause vasodilation via beta2-vascular receptors; large doses may produce constriction of skeletal and vascular smooth muscle. Metabolic effects include glycogenolysis in liver and skeletal muscle, mobilization of free fatty acids (FFA), increased plasma lactate, stimulation of the metabolic rate; increased myocardial excitability may lead to extrasystoles and serious arrhythmias.

stem: Which of the following mnemonic devices can be used to help the medical transport provider who is completing the primary assessment on a trauma patient who was injured when he fell from the top of a building while attempting to participate in parkour?
rationale: The medical transport provider will need to complete both a primary and secondary assessment during care of a trauma patient. The goal of the primary assessment is the evaluation of the patient's condition and identification of any factors which might prove to be life-threatening. The ABCDE mnemonic aids in ensuring each of the critical components of the primary assessment are completed: A: Assess for airway patency and stabilize the cervical spine; B: Assess breathing and ventilation; C: Assess the circulation and manage any hemorrhage; D: Assess the patient for disability (neurologic injury); E: Expose (undress) the patient to evaluate for any obscured, but life-threatening injuries. The mnemonics JumpSTART and SALT are used when triaging patients. The acronym eFAST stands for extended Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma and is part of the secondary assessment.

stem: Which of the following interventions would improve the effectiveness of mask ventilation?
options: Pressure of PPV can be increased to a maximum of 20 cm H2O if necessary
Reposition the patient's head into a "sniffing" orientation
Extend the patient's neck so the airway is open
Apply the mask loosely to the patient's face
answer: Pressure of PPV can be increased to a maximum of 20 cm H2O if necessary
rationale: MR. SOPA describes interventions to improve the effectiveness of mask ventilation: M: Mask is tightly applied to the face; R: Reposition the head into a 'sniffing' orientation; S: Suction the nares and the pharynx; O: Open the mouth; P: Pressure of PPV can be increased to a maximum of 40 cm H2O; A: Alternate airway plan and consideration.

stem: Providing high-quality, effective cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) in a moving ambulance or in an aircraft during a flight can be challenging. Resuscitation roles and responsibilities must be well-defined for effective and rapid response during an emergency. What is the primary role of the CPR team leader during transport?
rationale: Generally, the number of crew members available to perform basic and advanced life support resuscitation is limited to only two medically trained personnel; each team member, however, must take on specific roles and responsibilities. The team leader ensures that closed-loop communication occurs at all times. This requires any transport personnel to repeat orders and interventions and to announce the completion of the orders.

stem: The medical air transport crew is in flight with an asthmatic patient experiencing an acute asthma exacerbation. The crew prepares to intubate the patient using rapid sequence intubation (RSI). Which of the following induction agents is preferred for patients experiencing respiratory distress due to reactive airway complications?
rationale: Rapid sequence intubation (RSI) has largely replaced other methods of rapidly securing an emergency airway in the field. Patients should receive both sedatives and neuromuscular blocking agents (NMBAs) for RSI; this differs from simply sedating the patient in preparation for intubation. Patients with known reactive airway complications, including asthma exacerbations, benefit most from induction using ketamine. Ketamine possesses bronchodilating properties, improving the asthmatic patient's hemodynamic stability, while at the same time providing sedation for intubation so a stable airway can be ensured. Flumazinil is not an induction agent; it is a reversal agent used in reversing the effects of benzodiazepine drugs such as midazolam.

stem: Which of the following medications is a lipid-soluble induction agent used during intubation that combines rapid onset with rapid awakening?
rationale: Propofol produces unconsciousness and excellent amnesia within 30 seconds following a dose of 2 to 2.5 mg/kg. The awakening from propofol is more rapid and complete than from any other induction agent. It does, however, blunt compensatory tachycardia. Therefore, for patients with cardiac disease or hypovolemia, it may not be the best choice for induction. Ketamine is a phencyclidine derivative that produces dissociative amnesia. It is the only induction agent that provides both amnesia and analgesia. Etomidate is a barbiturate-like derivative without the adverse effects of barbiturates. Fentanyl is a pretreatment medication (not an induction agent) that is administered in situations where the sympathetic response to intubation must be controlled.

stem: You are transporting a patient who experienced a cerebrovascular accident (CVA) and is on a ventilator. You are assessing the patient's ventilatory status using a digital capnograph tool and find that the patient has an EtCO2 greater than 45 mmHg. Which of the following next steps should be taken to treat your patient's condition?
options: Increase the tidal volume and then increase the rate
answer: Increase the tidal volume and then increase the rate
rationale: Patients who have experienced CVA or other traumatic event which may damage the pons and upper medulla often develop respiratory acidosis, evidenced by an EtCO2 greater than 45 mmHg. For patients whose respiratory status is already being maintained on a ventilator, increasing the tidal volume followed by an increase in the rate of ventilation should aid in resolving the hypercarbic respiratory failure. Increasing the FiO2 and PEEP is appropriate in patients who are experiencing respiratory failure due to hypoxia, not hypercarbia. Disease processes such as acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), pneumonia, and congestive heart failure (CHF) all may result in the development of hypoxia.

stem: A medical air transport program has received a call requesting that a patient who is believed to have sustained a spinal cord injury in a motor vehicle accident in a rural area be picked up from the accident site and air lifted. According to the American College of Surgeons (ACS) recommendations on the transfer of injured patients, where should this patient be transported?
options: To a Level 2 hospital that has a top-notch emergency department located 50 miles away
To a Level 3 trauma center that has a reputation for excellence and a strong intensive care department located 75 miles away
To a community hospital located 15 miles away for stabilization and then further transport
To a Level 1 hospital that has a neurosurgical trauma center located 150 miles away
answer: To a Level 1 hospital that has a neurosurgical trauma center located 150 miles away
rationale: According to the American College of Surgeons (ACS) recommendations, it is no longer advisable to simply transfer or transport critical patients to the nearest located hospital or trauma center. Prior to the advanced and frequent use of medical air transport programs, typically critically ill or injured patients were simply transported to the closest medical facility, stabilized, and then often ultimately transferred out to a more appropriate facility. When at all possible, the medical air transport team should plan to stabilize critically ill or injured patients at the site where they are picked up, and then package and transport the patient to the trauma center or hospital most appropriate to care for the specific needs of the patient. Of particular concern is the appropriate transport or transfer of patients who have sustained spinal cord injuries; open skull fractures or penetration injuries to the skull; major abdominal, carotid, or other great vessel injury or bleed; serious liver injuries or unstable pelvic fractures which are likely to require ≥ 6 liters of blood in transfusion; a Glasgow Coma Scale score of less than 14, among many other critical injuries.

stem: When choosing a transport ventilator for a medical air transport program, which of the following ventilator battery types is best for use during medical air transport?
rationale: The best ventilator is only as good as its battery when it comes to medical air transport. Many medical air transport programs operate in large geographical areas, or are frequently required to fly missions over many hours, making a dependable ventilator battery a necessity. Ventilators may be equipped with lithium ion batteries, lead-acid batteries (similar to those used in vehicles), nickel-cadmium batteries, or nickel metal hydride batteries. Lithium ion batteries, while the most expensive of the battery types, is best for use during medical air transport missions due to their light weight, slow loss of charge (during storage), and no memory effect (which can ultimately cause rechargeable batteries to lose their ability to be charged).

stem: In which of the following patients should a nasopharyngeal airway not be utilized?
options: 27-year-old female with suspected head injury due to a fall
An 8-year-old male with severe stridor due to croup
answer: 27-year-old female with suspected head injury due to a fall
rationale: The nasopharyngeal airway is an acceptable means of establishing and maintaining an airway in patients requiring one. If the patient's airway reflexes are intact, they may demonstrate considerable gagging, vomiting, or develop laryngospasm as a result of placing either a nasopharyngeal or oral airway. When placing a nasopharyngeal airway in a pediatric patient, care must be taken to ensure the appropriate sized airway is utilized, and that the airway is liberally lubricated prior to insertion attempts in order to prevent damage to potentially hypertrophied adenoids, and subsequently, bleeding into the airway. The nasopharyngeal airway may also be used in patients who are comatose or who are stuporous and who require only minimal assistance with airway maintenance. Nasopharyngeal airways should not be placed in patients who are suspected of having sustained head injury, or who have obvious facial trauma.

stem: Which of the following supraglottic airway (SGA) devices does not use an inflatable cuff to obtain a seal of the esophagus and structures of the pharynx?
rationale: Supraglottic airway (SGA) devices, also often referred to interchangeably as laryngeal mask airway (LMA) devices, first came into use in the 1980s in the United Kingdom. The LMA, which is a type of SGA, is reusable, has an inflatable cuff, comes in sizes from neonate to adult, and provides an excellent alternative to endotracheal tube placement when this cannot be achieved, and is now also considered to be a first-line airway management strategy. The Combitube device and the King airway are both considered to be retroglottic devices (the tube is passed behind the larynx and the glottis, into the esophagus, with two balloon cuffs used to seal off the opening of the larynx), as opposed to supraglottic devices. Only the I-gel, a type of SGA, does not use an inflatable cuffed- or balloon system for sealing off the esophagus and pharynx. Instead, the I-gel is manufactured using a medical-grade thermoplastic polymer with a gel-like texture with a design intended to be snugly fit into the perilaryngeal structures.

stem: You are evaluating the airway of a patient being prepared for air transport. The patient has a long, thin neck, and when examining the patient's oral cavity, you are able to visualize the soft palate, the uvula, and both the anterior and posterior tonsillar pillars. What is the Mallampati score of this patient?
rationale: The Mallampati score is used to grade or classify the patient's airway in reference to ease or difficulty of endotracheal intubation. Individuals with a Mallampati score of I often are tall and have a long, thin neck, while individuals with a Mallampati score of IV often have a neck that is short, fat, or muscular. When examining the oral cavity of an individual with a Mallampati score of I, the examiner can easily visualize the soft palate, the uvula, and both the anterior and posterior tonsillar pillars. Patients with a Mallampati I score are typically easy to intubate. A Mallampati score of II indicates that while the soft palate and uvula were visible during the examination, view of the tonsillar pillars was obscured by the tongue. A Mallampati score of III indicates that when examining the oral cavity, only the soft palate and base of the uvula were able to be visualized. Patients with a Mallampati III score may present moderate difficulty during intubation. A Mallampati score of IV indicates that during examination of the oral cavity, only the soft palate was visible; both the uvula and anterior and posterior tonsillar pillars were fully hidden from view by the tongue. It can be quite difficult to establish an airway in these individuals.

stem: During the transport of an injured patient, hypothermia may be worsened by all the following causes, except:
options: Covering wet clothing with warm blankets to prevent heat loss
rationale: Hypothermia may be caused by the environment or by caregiver interventions during transport. When patients are undressed for assessment or infused with room-temperature IV fluids and refrigerated blood products, a significant drop in core temperature will follow if measures are not taken to prevent this occurrence. Removal of wet clothing, using warmed IV fluids, administering blood using a heated infuser, and keeping the transport vehicle or resuscitation bay at a warm temperature are all interventions that will prevent heat loss and decrease the risk of hypothermia.

stem: The patient you are transporting via medical air transport is being maintained on a ventilator using an assist-control (AC) ventilation mode. Which of the following complications is associated with use of AC ventilation during medical air transport?
rationale: The vibrations experienced during air transport are enough to cause an automatic triggering of the ventilator to deliver breaths when the ventilator is set to assist-control (AC) ventilation mode. This additional ventilator triggering can cause discomfort to the patient, air trapping, and stacking of breaths, otherwise referred to as 'auto-PEEP.' Should this occur, the patient is at greater risk of developing barotrauma or ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI), and the patient is required to work harder during breathing, as the force with which the patient breathes is decreased and less efficient.

stem: You are transporting an adult trauma patient with suspected intra-abdominal bleeding following a motor vehicle collision. The patient is hypotensive (BP 78/42), tachycardic, and has a declining level of consciousness. Based on current evidence and transport protocols, which prehospital resuscitation approach is most appropriate to initiate during transport?
options: Administer repeated 500 mL boluses of crystalloids until blood pressure normalizes
Initiate administration of packed red blood cell (PRBC) transfusion alone to improve tissue perfusion during transport.
Infuse plasma only to maintain intravascular volume and manage coagulopathy
Minimize crystalloid use and initiate balanced blood product transfusion if available, avoiding unbalanced resuscitation.
answer: Minimize crystalloid use and initiate balanced blood product transfusion if available, avoiding unbalanced resuscitation.
rationale: In hemorrhagic shock, the optimal prehospital resuscitation strategy is balanced transfusion using PRBCs, plasma, and platelets in a 1:1:1 ratio when available. This approach addresses volume loss, oxygen delivery, and coagulopathy simultaneously. If whole blood is unavailable, initiating balanced component therapy and minimizing crystalloid use is preferred. Excessive crystalloid administration can exacerbate coagulopathy, dilute clotting factors, and contribute to hypothermia and acidosis, worsening patient outcomes. Large-volume crystalloid boluses are associated with increased mortality and complications such as abdominal compartment syndrome and worsening coagulopathy. PRBCs alone address oxygen delivery but not clotting or plasma volume. Balanced resuscitation is superior. Plasma-only therapy does not address oxygen delivery or red cell volume. When available, it should be used in conjunction with PRBCs and platelets.

stem: A patient who is being transported by medical air transport sustained an injury which requires splinting. Which of the following statements about the use of splints during medical air transport is most accurate?
options: All splints should be supported by the use of blanket rolls once the patient has been loaded into the aircraft
Air-filled splints are most appropriate for use during medical air transport due to their more sleek profile
Traction splints should be applied after the patient has been packaged and loaded into the aircraft
Distal circulation should be monitored when using air-filled splints
answer: Distal circulation should be monitored when using air-filled splints
rationale: Air transport of patients typically occurs within confined and cramped spaces of air ambulances, posing challenges for the transport of patients who require the use of backboards, splints, or other immobilization devices. Even large dressings may complicate the loading, transport, and unloading of a patient being transported by air ambulance. The medical transport crew must take all necessary steps to ensure splints are not disrupted when transporting patients who require these devices, including providing support to splinted extremities through the use of any appropriate means (blanket rolls, pillows, egg crates, etc.). Air-filled splints or fixed traction splints may be utilized during medical air transport. If an air splint is utilized during medical air transport, the medical crew must monitor the distal circulation of the extremity, releasing air from the splint if needed due to an increase in pressure as altitude is reached.

stem: Your colleague is performing CPR compressions at a rate you consider too slow. What constructive feedback should you offer?
options: Compressions need to be at a rate of at least 100 per minute
Compressions need to be at a rate of 80 to 120 per minute
Compressions need to be at a rate of 100 to 120 per minute
Compressions need to be at a rate of at least 120 per minute
answer: Compressions need to be at a rate of 100 to 120 per minute
rationale: The critical lifesaving steps of BLS are prevention, immediate recognition and activation of the emergency response system, early high-quality CPR, and rapid defibrillation for shockable rhythms. High-quality CPR is a primary component in improving outcomes, and healthcare teams must be coordinated and competent working together effectively. High-quality CPR performance metrics include chest compression fraction > 80%, compression rate of 100-120/min, compression depth of at least 50 mm (2 inches) in adults and at least 1/3 the AP dimension of the chest in infants and children, and no excessive ventilation.

stem: During medical air transport of a patient that sustained traumatic injuries, the patient experiences pulseless ventricular tachycardia (pulseless V-tach). According to current ACLS guidelines, what should be the first-line treatment the medical flight crew administers to this patient?
rationale: Current (2015) ACLS guidelines recommend defibrillation for patients experiencing pulseless V-tach. The V-tach originates from an ectopic ventricular pacemaker, causing ventricular rates as high as 250 beats/minute. Patients experiencing V-tach may be classified as stable or unstable, and as having pulses or being pulseless. Stable patients are typically without the symptoms associated with unstable V-tach (chest pain, hypotension, decrease in consciousness, showing signs of cardiogenic shock, shortness of breath, pulmonary congestion), and should be treated with medications such as amiodarone, procainamide, and sotalol as first-line treatment agents. Unstable patients that have pulses and have ECG tracings revealing a wide, regular V-tach rhythm should be treated with immediate synchronized cardioversion (if the rhythm is irregular, the patient should receive immediate defibrillation instead of cardioversion). Pulseless patients should be immediately defibrillated; if the V-tach is refractory to defibrillation, amiodarone may be administered as a second-line treatment.

stem: What is normal cardiac output in an adult and how is it calculated?
options: 4-8 L/min, CO = heart rate x stroke volume
answer: 4-8 L/min, CO = heart rate x stroke volume
rationale: Normal cardiac output is 4-8 L/min and is calculated by multiplying heart rate by stroke volume. An easy way to remember this value is that minute volume is also 4-8 L/min in the respiratory system. Knowing that there is a ventilation to perfusion ratio to maintain, it is easy to see why these two values are the same.

stem: All the following interventions may be utilized to help prevent bleeding with the insertion of a nasotracheal tube, except:
options: Use a larger tube size for adequate patient ventilation
Apply gentle force when inserting the tube
Lubricate the tube with lidocaine jelly prior to insertion
Apply a vasoconstriction agent to the nasal mucosa prior to insertion
answer: Use a larger tube size for adequate patient ventilation
rationale: Nasal bleeding upon nasotracheal intubation may be prevented in the following ways: Use the correct sized tube (a tube too large will increase the risk of bleeding); generously lubricate the tube with lidocaine jelly prior to insertion; do not use excessive force with insertion; use a vasoconstrictive agent applied to the nasal mucosa prior to insertion.

stem: What is the next step after defibrillation attempt?
options: Determine if a carotid pulse is present
Check ECG for evidence of a rhythm
rationale: In its 2015 Guidelines, the American Heart Association and the European Resuscitation Council identify five critical components required to provide high-quality CPR, and the 2020 Guidelines reiterate the necessity of high-quality compressions, with a focus on eliminating delays between elements of CPR. Components of high-quality CPR include: hand position during compressions, chest compression rate, chest compression depth and chest wall recoil, compression-to-ventilation ratio, timing of rhythm check, and feedback for CPR quality.

stem: You are caring for a patient that goes into pulseless ventricular tachycardia while en route to the hospital. What is the first step in treating this patient?
rationale: Upon recognition of pulseless V-tach, the CFRN should immediately defibrillate. Care should be taken if this occurs while flying to avoid touching the rails of the stretcher and notification is given to the pilot prior to shock delivery. ACLS protocols then call for the giving of epinephrine if the patient remains pulseless, while chest compressions are being performed. Lidocaine is given if VT is refractory to amiodarone. Pacing the patient is done when a patient is having symptomatic bradycardia.

stem: A 49-year-old male who was accidentally shot with a hunting rifle at close-range by his teenage son while they were preparing to leave on a hunting trip was successfully resuscitated by the medical transport team and rapidly transported to a nearby trauma center. After several days of hospitalization in the intensive care unit, his condition, which had seemed to be improving, began to quickly deteriorate. Which of the following statements regarding multiple organ dysfunction is most accurate?
options: Multiple organ dysfunction most likely results from the occult presence of infectious organisms
Pulmonary embolism commonly occurs as a result of multiple organ dysfunction
The kidneys are typically the first organ to fail in multiple organ dysfunction
Multiple organ dysfunction typically affects two or more organ systems that were not involved in the initial insult
answer: Multiple organ dysfunction typically affects two or more organ systems that were not involved in the initial insult
rationale: Patients who were successfully resuscitated after experiencing a potentially catastrophic physiological insult commonly experience a severe worsening of their overall condition unrelated to their initial physiologic insult at some point during their critical care stay. This phenomenon has been termed multiple organ dysfunction (previously multiple organ failure but changed due to the propensity of the organs to improve with appropriate treatment), and typically presents as pulmonary failure, followed by failure of the kidneys, immune system, GI tract, and liver. It is believed that the multiple organ dysfunction results from a localized inflammatory response due to the initial injury, causing endothelial injury, activation of platelets and the clotting cascade, and the release of inflammatory mediators.

stem: A medical air transport program has consented to complete a mission transporting a patient on extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) to a transplant center. All of the following are necessary considerations when transporting a patient on ECMO, except:
options: Blood products must be readily available during the transport flight
spare ECMO circuit must be brought on the mission
perfusionist is required to accompany the patient during transport
cardiothoracic surgeon is required to accompany the patient during transport
answer: cardiothoracic surgeon is required to accompany the patient during transport
rationale: There are many necessary considerations which must be put in place prior to embarking on a medical transport mission of a patient on ECMO. Medical air programs may vary in how they will meet the critical needs of these patients, and there is some acceptable variability in how these needs may be met. Certain requirements, such as the need for a back-up ECMO circuit and blood products, are non-negotiable and must be present during each mission transporting patients on ECMO. Most transport crews also maintain a perfusionist (or at least ready access to a perfusionist) who will accompany each ECMO patient on transport flights. Occasionally this role will be taken over by a physician or nurse who has been specially trained in the perfusionist skills necessary for maintaining the patient on the ECMO machine. Most transport teams transporting patients on ECMO do not include a cardiothoracic surgeon, nor is this required.

stem: You are preparing to emergently intubate a patient during transport and begin passively oxygenating the patient. To do this, you should place a nasal cannula on the patient and begin an oxygen flow at what rate?
rationale: Passive oxygenation prior to emergency intubation capitalizes on the ability of hemoglobin to take up oxygen by diffusion; it can prolong the duration of safe hypoventilation without desaturation. The requirements for oxygen uptake are a patent airway for oxygen delivery and a high oxygen concentration gradient. Passive oxygenation can be achieved by using 15 L/min of nasal cannula oxygen as the patient becomes obtunded.

stem: You are reviewing the chest radiograph (CXR) obtained by the referring hospital prior to transport of the patient. Which of the following statements about reviewing CXR is correct?
options: rotated film can be identified by locating the distal ends of the clavicles in relation to the vertebral bodies
Using the PQR-ABCDEFGHI method can assist in accurate, thorough review of CXR
Heart size should encompass at least half of the thoracic diameter
An AP CXR will cause the heart and great vessels look slightly smaller
answer: Using the PQR-ABCDEFGHI method can assist in accurate, thorough review of CXR
rationale: Any radiographic studies that were obtained prior to transport should (ideally) be reviewed prior to medical air transport to aid the transport team in determining best delivery of care and potential for any exacerbation of the underlying condition during transport. The CXR can be useful in determining accuracy and positioning of therapeutic devices such as correct endotracheal tube (ETT) placement, central line position, or chest tube placement, as well as in evaluating underlying pathology. In order to ensure the CXR is reviewed thoroughly and systematically, the clinician can use the PQR-ABCDEFGHI method for reviewing the film(s). P: Position of the patient for the CXR. In an AP (anterior-posterior) film, the heart and great vessels will look larger, which limits accurate interpretation of cardiac or mediastinal concerns. Q: Quality of the CXR. R: Rotation of the film may obscure findings or landmarks; the easiest method of identifying film rotation is to look for the proximal ends of the clavicles in relation to the vertebral bodies. A: Airway - assess the position of the trachea and for any stenosis. B: Bones and tissue. C: Cardiac - the lobes of the right lung should take up less than half the diameter of the thorax. D: Diaphragm. E: Extraneous - note any lines or tubes present. F: Fields of the lungs. G: Gastric bubble should be located under the left hemidiaphragm. H: Hilum (the main bronchus and the pulmonary arteries). I: Impression.

stem: Rapid sequence intubation (RSI) frequently makes use of the following drug "cocktail" for completion of the procedure: fentanyl, ketamine, and succinylcholine. Which of the following potential side effects of these drugs is most likely caused by the administration of ketamine?
rationale: RSI has become the most common intubation method utilized when emergency intubation is required and requires the administration of a "cocktail" of drugs in order to safely complete the procedure. Typically, patients receive sedative agents, often an opioid (fentanyl is commonly used), an induction agent (ketamine in this instance), and a neuromuscular blocking agent (NMBA) (most often succinylcholine) during the delivery of RSI. Ketamine may cause an increase in secretions in the airway, as well as laryngospasm. The medical crew should make sure to have working suction on site to remove the secretions, and may need to consider the administration of either atropine or scopolamine if the use of suction does not fully resolve the issue. Fentanyl may cause the development of chest wall rigidity, typically when administered at higher doses. Succinylcholine can cause the development of hyperkalemia as a result of depolarization.

stem: All of the following statements about using straight or curved laryngoscope blades are true except:
options: The curved blade follows the natural curve of the oral cavity and pharynx
The flange of the straight blade allows for better manipulation of the endotracheal tube (ETT) on the right side of the mouth
The straight blade directly displaces the epiglottis
The straight blade is preferred for use when intubating infants or children
answer: The flange of the straight blade allows for better manipulation of the endotracheal tube (ETT) on the right side of the mouth
rationale: Both straight and curved laryngoscope blades continue to be used with regularity during intubation. The curved blade, also referred to as a Macintosh blade, is more commonly used when intubating adults, and lifts the epiglottis via the vallecula. It is the most useful of the two blade styles for controlling the tongue, and its flange allows for better manipulation of the ETT on the right side of the mouth. The curved blade follows the curve of the oral cavity and airway, also aiding ETT insertion. The straight blades, of which there are several, including the Miller, Phillips, or Wisconsin, are more frequently used when intubating newborns, infants, or children. This blade style allows the intubator the advantage of being able to view the glottic opening through direct displacement of the epiglottis in patients whose larynx is positioned more anteriorly (pediatric population). It is more difficult to use and does not control the tongue as well as the curved blade does.

stem: You obtain a chest radiograph of an intubated adult patient for endotracheal tube (ETT) placement confirmation. Which of the following identifies the correct depth of the ETT on X-ray?
options: At the level of the carina
rationale: On a chest X-ray, confirm the placement of the patient's ETT by ensuring the tube is located at the following anatomical locations: 5 cm above the carina (+/- 2 cm); at the level of T2-T4; at the junction of the collarbone. Waveform capnography is considered the gold standard for verification of proper endotracheal tube placement, but current guidelines caution that it is unreliable in low-perfusion states such as cardiac arrest (often, critical patients are in low-perfusion states).

stem:
You are called for an inter-facility transfer of a patient. Upon arrival the patient's nurse gives you the following

ABG results:
pH: 7.48
CO₂: 50
HCO₃: 28
PaO₂: 85
SaO₂: 96%
BE: 2

Which condition is the patient most likely in, and give the correct compensation status.
rationale: The pH on the ABG is 7.48, which means the patient is in alkalosis. Therefore, we can immediately eliminate any answer that states the patient is in acidosis. Now, we must determine if the cause is respiratory or metabolic in nature. The bicarbonate is high at 28 and therefore, we know that it is metabolic in nature. To determine compensation status we must now see if the CO2 is high, normal, or low. In this case, the CO2 is high and we know that the status is partially compensated.

stem: A Mallampati score of class II indicates which of the following?
options: Only the hard palate is visible.
Only the base of the uvula and soft palate are visible.
The soft palate, uvula, and fauces are visible.
The soft palate, uvula, fauces, and tonsillar pillars are visible.
answer: The soft palate, uvula, and fauces are visible.
rationale: The Mallampati score describes the ability to see structures in the oropharynx. It is a simple scoring system that relates the amount of mouth opening to the size of the tongue and provides an estimate of space available for oral intubation by direct laryngoscopy (DL). Intubation is typically not difficult in class I (soft palate, uvula, fauces, and tonsillar pillars are visible) and II (the tonsillar pillars disappear, but the soft palate, uvula, and fauces are visible) airways. In a class III airway (only the soft palate and base of the uvula are visible), DL is predicted to be moderately difficult. In a class IV airway (where only the hard palate is visible), DL is unlikely to be successful.

stem: You are the CFRN caring for a patient who requires rapid sequence intubation (RSI). This practice includes preoxygenation, administration of a predetermined induction drug dose, the use of neuromuscular blocking agents (NMBAs), and then tracheal intubation when relaxation has occurred. Which of the following is commonly used to counterbalance the cholinergic effects of succinylcholine?
rationale: Succinylcholine is the only NMBA in use; it has many contraindications, however, including significant bradycardia (especially in children, young adults, and patients given a repeat dose of succinylcholine). Atropine, an antiarrhythmic, is used to counterbalance the effects of this drug. Atropine increases the heart rate and improves atrioventricular conduction by blocking the parasympathetic influences on the heart. It also decreases oral secretions, which can be a benefit to the intubation specialist.

stem: You are a CFRN called to transport a 76-year-old male with history of pneumonia and ARDS to a higher level of care. The patient is intubated, and you and your partner confirm tube placement by auscultation of lung sounds and waveform capnography after moving him to your cot. The patient's ventilator settings are Tidal Volume 400mL, rate 12, FiO2 of 0.50, and PEEP of 5 cm H2O. You note that the patient's SpO2 is is 87%. What is the likely cause and the most correct course of action?
options: Hypercarbic Respiratory Failure; Increase FiO2 and increase PEEP
Hypoxic Respiratory Failure; Increase Tidal Volume and increase rate
Hypercarbic Respiratory Failure; Increase Tidal Volume and increase rate
Hypoxic Respiratory Failure; Increase FiO2 and increase PEEP
answer: Hypoxic Respiratory Failure; Increase FiO2 and increase PEEP
rationale: In cases with patients suffering from pneumonia and/or ARDS, Hypoxic Respiratory Failure is more likely than Hypercarbic Respiratory Failure. This is because the patient has a decreased ability to diffuse oxygen due to atelectasis of the alveoli. The treatment for this would be to increase the FiO2 and increase PEEP to allow more oxygen and keep the alveoli open with increased PEEP.

stem: An adult patient opens his eyes when they hear you shouting for help, groans and makes unintelligible sounds, and there is no response to pain. What is their GCS score?
rationale: The purpose of the GCS (Glasgow Coma Scale/Score) is to describe and communicate the condition of an individual patient by separate, multidimensional ratings of their eye, verbal, and motor responses. After 40 years, it remains the appropriate method for this purpose. It is calculated by addition of the total points selected under each component (eye, verbal, motor) for a total of 15 possible points. For this patient: Eye opening to verbal command = E3; Unintelligible sounds (groaning) = V2; No motor response to pain = M1. Total GCS = 6 (M1, V2, E3). Severity of injury based on GCS score: 14--15: Mild; 9--13: Moderate; 3--8: Severe.

stem: Which of the following answers best describes the following ABG? pH- 7.49, CO2- 33, HCO3- 21, PaO2- 80, SaO2- 80, BE +1
rationale: First look at the pH. Here we see a pH of 7.49 telling us the patient is alkalotic. Immediately, we can eliminate any answer choice stating acidosis. Moving on to the cause of the alkalosis we see a low CO2 and a low bicarbonate. Remember the inverse relationship between CO2 and pH; if the alkalosis was respiratory in nature, we would see a low CO2 with a high pH. This is what we see in our ABG, so we can determine the cause is respiratory, and we can now eliminate choices stating metabolic. Now, determine compensation state. The compensatory mechanism for the respiratory system is the metabolic system. In the ABG, we see a low bicarbonate, telling us the metabolic system is attempting to compensate for the high pH. Therefore, it is partially compensated.

stem: You are preparing to participate in the rapid sequence intubation (RSI) of a patient being transported via medical air transport. All of the following "P's" should be followed to guarantee successful intubation except:
options: Position all patients with a pad behind the shoulders
Prepare by making sure all equipment is in working order
Preoxygenate the patient prior to attempting intubation
Use a paralytic agent during RSI ✗
answer: Position all patients with a pad behind the shoulders
